my_sentence: I have a higher interest about writing code.
correction: I have a higher interest in writing code.
explanation: The noun 'interest' takes the preposition 'in', not 'about', when naming the thing you are interested in: 'an interest in X'. (assistant note)
correct_examples: She has a strong interest in photography.
He showed little interest in the project.
context: Talking about level of interest in his own job (writing code)

my_sentence: I prefer continue doing it until I retire.
correction: I prefer to continue doing it until I retire.
explanation: 'Prefer' is followed by a to-infinitive (or a gerund), so it must be 'prefer to continue', not 'prefer continue'.
correct_examples: I prefer to walk to work.
They prefer to stay home on weekends.
context: Saying he wants to keep writing code until retirement

my_sentence: It can give me a kind of achievement.
correction: It can give me a sense of achievement.
explanation: The fixed collocation is 'a sense of achievement' (the feeling of having accomplished something), not 'a kind of achievement'.
correct_examples: Finishing the marathon gave her a real sense of achievement.
There's a great sense of achievement in solving a hard problem.

my_sentence: I don't have any opportunity to communicate with them, so I can't show my respect with them.
correction: I don't have any opportunity to communicate with them, so I can't show my respect to them.
explanation: You 'show respect to' someone (the recipient takes 'to'), not 'show respect with' them. 'With' would imply doing it together.
correct_examples: Students should show respect to their teachers.
He always shows respect to his elders.
context: Why he can't say how much he respects a professor's job

my_sentence: Compare to some people who live in the big city, my salary is lower.
correction: Compared to some people who live in big cities, my salary is lower.
explanation: Use the past participle 'compared to' (a reduced passive) to introduce a comparison, not the bare verb 'compare to'. (assistant note)
correct_examples: Compared to last year, sales have doubled.
Compared to my old phone, this one is much faster.
context: Comparing his salary with people in big cities

my_sentence: I think it can be worse.
explanation: 'Worth it' (worthwhile / a good trade-off) and 'worse' (more bad) are different words; he meant the high salary made the hard life worthwhile, so the phrase is 'worth it'.
correct_examples: The job is stressful, but the pay makes it worth it.
It was a long trip, but the view was worth it.
context: Whether a professor's busy life is worth the high salary

my_sentence: It's the 25th in the GDP in my country.
correction: It is in the 25th place when it comes to the GDP.
explanation: Use 'in the Nth place' to give a ranking, and 'when it comes to X' to introduce the criterion, rather than 'the 25th in the GDP'.
correct_examples: Our team finished in the third place.
When it comes to cost, this option is the best.
context: Yantai's GDP ranking among Chinese cities

my_sentence: Working conditions means the condition of your office or working places.
correction: Working conditions refer to the state of your workplace.
explanation: 'Conditions' is plural, so it takes 'refer' not 'means'; and when defining a term, don't repeat the word itself ('conditions means the condition') — use 'refer to the state of'.
correct_examples: Working conditions refer to the environment in which you work.
These terms refer to two different things.

my_sentence: Most people you meet, you are familiar with them.
correction: You are familiar with most of the people you meet.
explanation: Don't add a resumptive pronoun ('them') at the end of a relative clause; the object is already the head noun. Also use 'most of the people' before a defining clause.
correct_examples: You are familiar with most of the people you meet there.
I know most of the songs they play.
context: Why the office feels secure — you know most people

my_sentence: I went to the Tokyo office many years ago, and I find that they have better conditions than us.
correction: I went to the Tokyo office many years ago, and I found that they had better conditions than us.
explanation: The event is in the past ('many years ago'), so the reporting verb must be past 'found', and the reported clause shifts to 'had' for sequence of tenses.
correct_examples: I visited the factory last year and found that it was very modern.
She called and said she was on her way.
context: Comparing the Tokyo office's working conditions

my_sentence: Even you are a manager, you can't earn much more than your members.
correction: Even if you are a manager, you can't earn much more than your team members.
explanation: To express a hypothetical/concessive condition you need 'even if', not just 'even', before a clause. (assistant note)
correct_examples: Even if you work hard, the salary stays the same.
Even if it rains, we'll go.
context: In Japan, salaries are similar regardless of position

my_sentence: You can go anyone in five or 10 min.
correction: You can go anywhere in five or ten minutes.
explanation: Use 'anywhere' (any place) for a destination, not 'anyone' (any person); also spell out 'minutes' as a plural noun.
correct_examples: From here you can get anywhere in ten minutes.
You can go anywhere you like.
context: Shopping malls being close to the Tokyo office

my_sentence: So people can easy to buy things.
correction: So people can easily go there and buy something.
explanation: After a modal ('can') use a bare verb modified by an adverb ('easily go / buy'), not 'easy to buy'. 'Easy' is an adjective and can't modify a verb here.
correct_examples: You can easily find it online.
People can easily get there by bus.
context: Stores on the first floor make shopping convenient

correction: We never experience earthquakes. / We never have earthquakes.
explanation: You 'experience' or 'have' a natural disaster, not 'meet' it; and 'earthquake' is countable, so use the plural 'earthquakes' for the general case.
correct_examples: This region rarely experiences earthquakes.
We sometimes have typhoons in summer.
context: His city doesn't have natural disasters

my_sentence: If you have a big rainfall, it will go to the sea.
correction: If there is a heavy rainfall, it usually goes down to the sea.
explanation: Rain intensity collocates with 'heavy', not 'big' ('heavy rainfall'); use existential 'there is' for weather events rather than 'you have'. ('rain' itself is uncountable — 'a heavy rainfall' is fine but not 'a rain').
correct_examples: If there is heavy rainfall, the river rises quickly.
We had heavy rain all night.
context: His city is near the sea, so rain drains away

my_sentence: At the noon, it's more than 30 degrees Celsius.
correction: At noon, it's more than 30 degrees Celsius.
explanation: 'Noon' is used without an article in time expressions: 'at noon' (compare 'at night', 'at midnight'). (assistant note)
correct_examples: We'll meet at noon.
The shop closes at midnight.
context: Big temperature swings in high-altitude areas

explanation: A specific, identifiable noun needs the definite article: 'the height' (of that place). 'Because of height' as a bare noun is ungrammatical here.
correct_examples: The flight was delayed because of the weather.
It's cold up there because of the altitude.
context: Why the temperature varies so much in Tibet

explanation: Use the adjective 'mountainous' (full of mountains) to describe a place, not the noun 'mountain' as a modifier here.
correct_examples: It's a mountainous region with few roads.
Many mountainous areas are hard to farm.
context: Much of western China is mountainous
